response:
# Verdict: REJECT ## Overall Assessment This tooltip component has **critical accessibility design gaps** that prevent keyboard users from accessing its content and screen reader users from understanding its purpose. The role="tooltip" is present but insufficient without proper association, focus management, and state communication. This pattern fails WCAG 2.1.1 Keyboard (keyboard operability) and WAI-ARIA Tooltip/Popover patterns. ## Pre-commitment Predictions Before reviewing the code: - **Predicted**: Missing `aria-describedby` to associate tooltip with trigger button ✓ Found - **Predicted**: Tooltip only appears on mouse hover, not focus ✓ Found - **Predicted**: No keyboard activation mechanism (Enter/Space) for showing/hiding tooltip ✓ Found - **Predicted**: Focus management undefined when tooltip opens/closes ✓ Found All predictions confirmed. The component has fundamental design flaws that pass automated tests but fail real users with assistive technology or keyboards-only navigation. ## Critical Findings ### 1. Tooltip Not Associated With Trigger Button via aria-describedby **File:** `src/components/BuggyTooltip.tsx:8-9` **Element:** `
// MISSING ``` - **User group impacted:** Screen reader users (NVDA, JAWS, VoiceOver) - **WCAG/APG citation:** WCAG 1.3.1 Info and Relationships; WAI-ARIA Tooltip Pattern - **Expected behavior:** Button should have `aria-describedby` pointing to tooltip element's id when tooltip is visible - **Actual behavior:** No association exists between trigger button and tooltip content **Why this matters:** Screen reader users hear the button text but never know a related description/tooltip exists. They cannot discover or navigate to it programmatically without mouse hover (which screen readers don't support). This blocks access to important contextual information. ### 2. Tooltip Only Appears on Mouse Hover, Not Focus **File:** `src/components/BuggyTooltip.tsx:7-10` **Element:** Button event handlers at line 8 ```jsx onMouseEnter={() => setShowTooltip(true)} onMouseLeave={() => setShowTooltip(false)} // NO onFocus/onBlur handlers ``` - **User group impacted:** Keyboard-only users, screen reader users relying on focus states - **WCAG/APG citation:** WCAG 2.1.1 Keyboard; WAI-ARIA Tooltip/Popover Pattern - **Expected behavior:** Tooltip should appear when button receives keyboard focus (Enter or Space), and hide when focus leaves via `onFocus`/`onBlur` handlers - **Actual behavior:** Only mouse hover triggers tooltip visibility **Why this matters:** WCAG 2.1.1 requires all functionality to be available without a mouse. Keyboard users cannot access the tooltip content at all because there's no way to trigger it with Enter or Space, and focus events don't change state. This is a complete keyboard trap for contextual information. ### 3. No Focus Management When Tooltip Opens/Closes **File:** `src/components/BuggyTooltip.tsx:10-24` **Element:** Tooltip div at line 15 ```jsx
// NO focus trap, no aria-modal handling {content}
``` - **User group impacted:** Keyboard users navigating complex interfaces with multiple tooltips - **WCAG/APG citation:** WAI-ARIA Tooltip Pattern (focus should move to tooltip when opened) - **Expected behavior:** When tooltip opens via focus, keyboard focus should transfer to the tooltip element. When closed, return focus to trigger button **Why this matters:** Without proper focus management, screen reader users may announce "tooltip" but cannot navigate into it or know where they are in the interface flow after closing a tooltip. This violates WCAG 2.4.3 Focus Order and creates confusion about current context. ### 4. Button May Have Unclear Accessible Name When Tooltip Is Sole Contextual Info **File:** `src/components/BuggyTooltip.tsx:7-9` **Element:** ` // No aria-label if trigger text is unclear or decorative icon-only button ``` - **User group impacted:** Screen reader users, low vision users relying on focus indicators alone - **WCAG/APG citation:** WCAG 4.1.2 Name, Role, Value; WAI-ARIA Tooltip Pattern (button must have accessible name) - **Expected behavior:** Button should either: (a) Have clear text content that stands as its own label, OR (b) Use `aria-label` if trigger is an icon or unclear text **Why this matters:** If the button only contains an icon with no visible text and relies on tooltip for context, screen reader users hear "button" followed by nothing. The tooltip doesn't help because it's not associated via aria-describedby. This violates WCAG 4.1.2 — buttons must have accessible names independent of dynamic content that appears conditionally. ## Major Findings ### 5. Tooltip Content Not Announced to Screen Readers When Visible **File:** `src/components/BuggyTooltip.tsx:10-24` **Element:** Tooltip div at line 15 ```jsx
// No aria-live region, no announcement mechanism {content}
``` - **User group impacted:** Screen reader users who rely on live announcements for dynamic content changes - **WCAG/APG citation:** WCAG 4.1.3 Status Messages; WAI-ARIA Tooltip Pattern (tooltip should be announced when shown) - **Expected behavior:** When tooltip becomes visible, screen readers should announce the content via aria-live="polite" or by ensuring it's in a region that gets naturally read **Why this matters:** The role="tooltip" alone doesn't make content accessible to AT. Screen reader users need either: (a) An association so they can navigate directly to tooltip when button is focused, OR (b) A live announcement mechanism. Currently neither exists — the tooltip only appears visually on mouse hover which screen readers don't support. ### 6. No Focus Indicator for Tooltip Element When Visible **File:** `src/components/BuggyTooltip.tsx:10-24` **Element:** Tooltip div at line 15 ```jsx
// CSS likely hides outline, no focus-visible handling {content}
``` - **User group impacted:** Low vision users relying on visual focus indicators; keyboard-only navigation verification - **WCAG/APG citation:** WCAG 2.4.7 Focus Visible (3:1 contrast perimeter) - **Expected behavior:** When tooltip is focused, it must have a visible outline meeting minimum requirements **Why this matters:** Even if we fix the association and focus management above, low vision users need to know which element currently has keyboard focus. If CSS removes outlines globally or doesn't provide `focus-visible` styles for non-native elements, they cannot verify their navigation position in complex tooltip-heavy interfaces. ## Minor Findings ### 7. Tooltip Should Use aria-hidden When Not Visible (WCAG 1.4.2) **File:** `src/components/BuggyTooltip.tsx:10-24` **Element:** Conditional rendering at line 15 ```jsx {showTooltip && (
)} // Tooltip not marked aria-hidden when hidden, but this is acceptable for conditional content ``` - **User group impacted:** Screen reader users who might hear "tooltip div" even when visually hidden (depending on AT behavior) - **WCAG/APG citation:** WCAG 1.4.2 Audio Control; general accessibility best practice - **Expected behavior:** When tooltip is not shown
